items.title: Bankart in anterior dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Bankart in anterior dislocation

  • Bankart injury: disinsertion of the anteroinferior glenoid rim due to avulsion of the labrum, with or without fracture of the glenoid rim (Bankart fracture).
  • Mechanism: fall with arm in abduction and forced external rotation, which dislocates the humeral head anteriorly and tears the labrum.
  • Bankart lesion is the most common cause of recurrent anterior instability of the shoulder, and its arthroscopic repair is the surgical standard in young athletic patients.
  • Diagnosis: arthro-MRI or arthroscopy; Conventional MRI has low sensitivity to detect the lesion.

items.title: cleft sign
items.contentHtml:
Groove sign in posterior dislocation

  • Groove sign: Palpable depression on the anteromedial aspect of the shoulder when the arm is in internal rotation, due to impact of the humeral head against the posterior glenoid rim.
  • It is observed in posterior dislocation of the shoulder, which represents <5% of all dislocations. glenohumeral, with a high rate of late diagnosis (up to 50% in emergencies).
  • Mechanism: violent contraction (epilepsy, electrocution) or previous direct trauma; the head moves posteriorly and becomes embedded in the posterior glenoid rim.
  • Associated radiological finding: light bulb sign (humeral head in symmetrical internal rotation) on anteroposterior radiograph.

items.title: Hill-Sachs and Bankart
items.contentHtml:
Hill-Sachs injury and Bankart fracture

  • Hill-Sachs injury: impaction fracture of the posterolateral portion of the humeral head against the anteroinferior glenoid rim during anterior dislocation.
  • The Hill-Sachs lesion may be engaging if the bone defect rotates and engages the rim. glenoid with the arm in abduction and external rotation, indicating significant instability and increased risk of recurrence.
  • Bankart fracture: fracture of the anterior inferior glenoid rim associated with the Bankart lesion; Both lesions are part of the glenohumeral instability triad (Bankart lesion, Hill-Sachs lesion and labral lesion).
  • Evaluation: CT scan with 3D reconstruction to quantify glenoid bone loss (>20% indicates Latarjet surgery) and the size of the humeral defect.

items.title: Axillary nerve in dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Axillary nerve in shoulder dislocation

  • Axillary nerve (circumflex): branch of the posterior fascicle of the brachial plexus (roots C5-C6); innervates the deltoid muscle and the teres minor, and gives sensitivity to the lateral aspect of the shoulder (area of the uniform patch).
  • It is the most common neurological injury in anterior glenohumeral dislocation (10-25% of cases), due to stretching or direct compression of the nerve against the displaced humeral head.
  • Clinical evaluation: check sensitivity in the lateral aspect of the shoulder and shoulder abduction strength (deltoid). before and after reduction; electromyography is reserved if there is no recovery after 3-4 weeks.
  • Most lesions are transient neurapraxias; spontaneous recovery occurs in weeks to months; Surgical exploration is indicated if there are no signs of reinnervation at 6 months.

items.title: Jobe test
items.contentHtml:
Jobe test for supraspinatus

  • Jobe test (empty can test): patient with both arms abducted at 90° in the scapular plane (30° horizontal flexion) and maximum internal rotation (thumbs toward the ground). The examiner applies downward pressure; the test is positive if pain or weakness appears.
  • Evaluates the integrity of the tendon of the supraspinatus muscle (first 30° of abduction).
  • Approximate sensitivity 80% and specificity 90% for complete tears of the supraspinatus; in partial tears the sensitivity is less.
  • Variant: full can test (thumbs up) reduces subacromial impingement and may be more tolerable, but with less sensitivity if there is pain associated with impingement.

items.foco: Arm drop sign in cuff rupture
items.title: Arm drop sign
items.contentHtml:
Cuff rupture drop arm sign

  • Drop arm sign: The examiner passively abducts the patient's arm to 90° and then asks the patient to actively maintain it; If the arm drops suddenly or the patient cannot maintain abduction, the test is positive.
  • It indicates massive tear of the rotator cuff, especially of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, with inability to initiate or maintain abduction against gravity.
  • The sign has high specificity (>95%) but low sensitivity, since it only appears in complete tears and extensive.
  • Differential diagnosis: axillary nerve injury (loss of deltoid strength) or paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle (winged scapula).

items.title: Gerber test
items.contentHtml:
Gerber test for subscapularis

  • Gerber test (lift-off test): patient places the hand on the affected side in the lumbar region (back of the hand against the back) and then tries to separate the hand from the back (active internal rotation). The test is positive if you cannot raise your hand.
  • It evaluates the integrity of the tendon of the subscapularis muscle (internal rotator of the shoulder).
  • Variant: belly-press test (abdominal compression test) when the patient cannot reach the lumbar region due to stiffness or pain; Press the abdomen with the hand and observe if the elbow falls backwards (positive if there is compensation with shoulder extension).
  • The Gerber test has sensitivity 60-70% and specificity >90% for complete tears of the subscapularis.

items.title: SLAP injury
items.contentHtml:
SLAP injury in instability

  • SLAP injury (Superior Labrum from Anterior to Posterior): tear of the glenoid labrum in its superior portion, from anterior to posterior, which compromises the insertion of the tendon of the long head of the biceps.
  • Mechanism: traction trauma to the biceps (weight lifting, overhead throwing) or glenohumeral dislocation.
  • Snyder classification: type I (degenerative), type II (disinsertion of the superior labrum and biceps, the most common and unstable), type III (bucket handle), type IV (extension to the bicipital tendon).
  • Diagnosis: arthro-MRI; The active compression test (O'Brien) is clinical: pain with the arm in 90° flexion, adduction and internal rotation, which improves with external rotation.

items.title: Dislocation triad
items.contentHtml:
Dislocation triad: age, epilepsy, electricity

  • Posterior shoulder dislocation triad: age (young, males), epilepsy (massive muscle contraction during tonic-clonic seizures) and electricity (electrocution, electric shock).
  • Posterior dislocation due to epilepsy or Electrocution is usually bilateral (up to 50% of cases).
  • Alcoholism is also included as a risk factor for falls and muscle relaxation, although it is not part of the classic triad.
  • In anterior dislocation, the classic triad does not apply; The typical mechanism is a fall with the arm in abduction and external rotation.

items.title: Hawkins maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Hawkins maneuver for subacromial impingement

  • Hawkins maneuver (Hawkins-Kennedy): patient in a sitting position, the examiner flexes the shoulder and elbow to 90°, then forcibly internally rotates the shoulder. The test is positive if it reproduces pain on the anterolateral aspect of the shoulder.
  • It evaluates subacromial impingement (impingement syndrome) by compressing the supraspinatus tendon and the subacromial bursa against the acromion and the coracoacromial ligament.
  • Sensitivity 80-90% and specificity 30-60%; a positive test suggests impingement but does not confirm a cuff tear.
  • Other impingement tests: Neer (passive elevation of the arm in internal rotation) and Yocum (hand on the contralateral shoulder and elevation of the elbow).

items.foco: Acute phase: closed reduction and sling
items.title: Acute phase: reduction and sling
items.contentHtml:
Acute phase: closed reduction and sling

  • Closed reduction: first line in acute glenohumeral dislocation; It is performed with analgesia (opioids, benzodiazepines or intra-articular block with 1% lidocaine) and sedation if necessary.
  • Most commonly used techniques: Stimson method (patient in prone position, downward arm traction), Hippocrates method (axial traction with counterfoot in the axilla, risk of neurovascular injury) and Milch technique (abduction and gentle external rotation).
  • Post-reduction: immobilization with sling (sling) in neutral rotation for 3-4 weeks in young patients to allow healing of the labrum; in people over 40 years of age, brief immobilization (1-2 weeks) to avoid stiffness.
  • Confirm reduction with anteroposterior x-ray and check pulse and distal sensitivity (axillary nerve).

items.foco: Surgery criteria for rotator cuff injury
items.title: Rotator cuff surgery
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for surgery in rotator cuff injury

  • Absolute surgical indications: complete tear of the rotator cuff in a young patient (<40 years) with acute trauma; massive tear with loss of function (positive arm drop sign); lack of improvement after 4-6 months of conservative treatment (physiotherapy, anti-inflammatories, activity modification).
  • Relative indications: partial tear with persistent pain; tear in throwing athletes; significant muscle atrophy on MRI (Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty degeneration).
  • Conservative treatment is first line in patients >65 years with chronic tear and acceptable function (active abduction >90°).
  • Arthroscopic repair is the standard; The open technique is reserved for massive or retracted tears.
